narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of first-time success on the bar exam, based on profiles from thousands of past students. The model looked at your academic record, test scores, family background, and other details to forecast your bar exam outcome. Unfortunately, it predicted a 92% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is the primary reason your application was rejected—law schools use this to prioritize candidates with stronger predicted odds of passing and practicing successfully. The three biggest factors pushing the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 school decile ranking, in that order of influence. Your Year 3 decile of 1—far below the typical student's 5.5—stood out as the strongest signal, suggesting your later undergraduate performance placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers, which strongly nudged the model toward expecting bar exam struggles. Similarly, your LSAT score of 26, well under the average of about 37, reinforced this concern by indicating potential challenges with the rigorous reasoning needed for law and the bar. Even your Year 1 decile of 1, again much lower than the average around 5.7, added to the risk, showing early academic hurdles that didn't improve. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, whose profiles often feature mid-to-high deciles and LSATs in the mid-30s or above, your metrics across these areas aligned more closely with those who faced first-try failures. Your upper-middle family income and full-time student status were more typical and didn't sway the outcome much. While this is a tough outcome, it's not a final verdict on your potential—many students overcome similar starts through targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your GPA with additional coursework or a post-bac program, and seeking mentorship from Asian American law professionals who navigated parallel paths to bar success. Reapplying after addressing these core areas could significantly shift your predicted odds.
